turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Thomas has the opinion of the court this morning in Case 23-825, Delligatti v. United States.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on a writ of certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit. §924(c) of Title 18 subjects any person who uses a firearm during or in relation to a crime of violence to a five-year mandatory minimum sentence. As relevant here, a crime of violence is defined as any crime that necessarily involves the use, attempted use, or threatened use of physical force. Salvatore Delligatti was charged with violating 924(c) after he recruited members of a gang to kill a suspected police informant. His indictment identified attempted murder under New York law as a predicate crime of violence. Delligatti moved to dismiss the charge arguing that attempted murder is not a crime of violence. He explained that murder can be committed under New York law by omission of a legal duty and he contended that in such cases, the offender does not use force. The District Court rejected that argument and the Second Circuit affirmed. In an opinion filed with the clerk today, we also affirm. It is impossible to deliberately cause bodily injury or death whether by act or omission without using physical force within the meaning of §924(c). As we held in Castleman v. United States, an offender uses force whenever he makes force his instrument regardless of whether he applies force directly or indirectly. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion today, we affirm the judgment of the Second Circuit. Justice Gorsuch has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Jackson joins.
